..summary: ExploreResponseAssembler is a static-methods-only final class bundling all exploration presentation logic into one flat namespace

evidence: final class ExploreResponseAssembler with private no-arg constructor and zero instance state; all methods are static.

285 lines covering: buildExplorationEdges, buildComparisonEdges, buildSingleSeedExploreResponse, buildMultiSeedExploreResponse, buildComparisonResponse, buildExamplesResponse, four private sub-entry builders, and an inner ExamplesContext record — all exploration-related but spanning edge construction and four distinct response shapes.

Callers (ExplorationHandlers) call ExploreResponseAssembler.buildSingleSeedExploreResponse(...) and ExploreResponseAssembler.buildComparisonResponse(...) at static call sites — the assembler cannot be specialised, mocked at a dependency boundary, or replaced per response type.
detail.suggestion: Split into two smaller focused classes: ExplorationEdgeBuilder (handling buildExplorationEdges, buildComparisonEdges) and ExploreResponseAssembler retaining the response-assembly factory methods. Alternatively convert ExploreResponseAssembler into a proper instance class that ExplorationHandlers holds as a field — this makes the dependency surface visible and allows future specialisation without changing callers.

..summary: RelationConfig and QueryExecutor javadoc still reference the non-existent RelationPatternUtils class after its merger into RelationUtils

evidence: RelationConfig.java line 15: 'Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in {@link RelationPatternUtils}' — RelationPatternUtils does not exist; methods live in RelationUtils.
QueryExecutor.java line 88: '{@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.RelationPatternUtils#buildFullPattern}' — stale qualified reference to a class that was merged away.
RelationPatternUtilsTest.java @DisplayName is 'RelationPatternUtils' but the class imports and tests RelationUtils — the test was updated to use the merged class but the display name was not updated.
detail.suggestion: Update RelationConfig.java javadoc to reference RelationUtils instead of RelationPatternUtils. Fix the {@link} reference in QueryExecutor.java to point to RelationUtils#buildFullPattern. Update RelationPatternUtilsTest @DisplayName to 'RelationUtils (pattern building)'.

..summary: QueryExecutor class-level 'Method responsibilities' block duplicates individual method Javadocs

evidence:
The interface Javadoc contains a '

Method responsibilities

'
    that describes all four methods, including distinguishing notes ('Distinct from executeCqlQuery') that are already present in the respective method Javadocs.
executeCqlQuery is described at class level ('Returns raw KWIC results without ranking') and again in its own Javadoc and @implNote, so a developer reads the same information in three places.
detail.suggestion: Remove the 'Method responsibilities' <ul> from the class-level Javadoc. The interface-level comment should explain the design rationale (why two ports exist, who should declare which) without restating per-method behaviour that the method Javadocs cover. The cross-method distinction notes belong inside the individual method Javadocs where they already appear.

..summary: Two Javadocs reference non-existent RelationPatternUtils class after apparent rename/merge

evidence: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern @param bcqlPattern: 'typically by the caller via RelationPatternUtils#buildFullPattern' — RelationPatternUtils does not exist; the utility lives in RelationUtils.
RelationConfig class Javadoc: 'Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in RelationPatternUtils' — same ghost reference. The logic actually lives in RelationUtils.
detail.suggestion: Replace both 'RelationPatternUtils' references with '{@link RelationUtils}'. In RelationConfig, update the Javadoc to reference the actual class. In QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern, update the @param cross-reference or remove the 'typically by the caller via ...' clause if it no longer accurately describes the usage.

..summary: ExplorationService sibling methods use different options types without symmetric justification at the call site

evidence: exploreByRelation accepts SingleSeedExplorationOptions(ExplorationOptions base, int reverseExpansionLimit) while exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles accept ExplorationOptions directly.

The rationale (reverseExpansionLimit is single-seed-only) is documented in SingleSeedExplorationOptions and the interface Javadoc, but a caller discovering the interface cold must read two files to understand why the three sibling methods have non-parallel signatures.
The open finding status confirms this was previously flagged and remains unresolved.
detail.suggestion: No code change is strictly required given the existing documentation is clear. If reducing API surprise is desired, consider overloading ExplorationService.exploreByRelation to accept a plain ExplorationOptions and resolve the default reverseExpansionLimit internally, so all three methods share the same options base type at their call sites.

..summary: ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method returns semantically different data per implementation

evidence: The sealed interface declares `List<DiscoveredNounEntry> primarySeeds()`. In SingleSeed it returns reverse-discovered nouns (serialised as `discovered_nouns`). In MultiSeed it returns caller-supplied seed words (serialised as `source_seeds`) and is annotated @JsonIgnore.
A developer calling `exploreResponse.primarySeeds()` polymorphically gets reverse-discovered nouns for one variant and input seeds for the other — opposing concepts sharing one method name.
The MultiSeed Javadoc acknowledges the divergence, but the interface method is still present and callable, making the interface contract misleading.
detail.suggestion: Remove primarySeeds() from the ExploreResponse sealed interface (it is not consumed polymorphically — ExploreResponseAssembler always constructs concrete types and HttpApiUtils accepts Object). The @JsonIgnore override in MultiSeed is a symptom: if the method were truly part of the shared contract it would not need to be ignored in one variant. Keeping the concrete discoveredNouns() and sourceSeeds() accessors on their respective records preserves type safety without the misleading shared method.

..summary: CollocateQueryHelper.forTesting() creates an instance with null index; type system cannot enforce the verbal-only usage contract

evidence: Primary constructor: CollocateQueryHelper(@NonNull BlackLabIndex index) — @NonNull signals index is always valid.
Private no-arg constructor: this.index = null — directly contradicts @NonNull.
forTesting() factory: returns new CollocateQueryHelper() with index=null.

Javadoc warns 'Any method that delegates to the BlackLab index will throw NullPointerException if called on this instance'; this contract is verbal only — the type system cannot distinguish a test instance from a production one.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a narrow test-only interface or subclass that overrides only the purely computational methods used in tests, leaving index-dependent methods unimplemented/throwing UnsupportedOperationException with a clear message. Alternatively, pass the index as Optional<BlackLabIndex> or use a guard method (assertIndexAvailable()) with a descriptive IllegalStateException instead of a raw NPE, so that miscalling a test instance produces a clear error rather than an opaque NullPointerException.

..summary: @NonNull seeds annotation contradicted by explicit null-check in exploreMultiSeed body

evidence: Method signature: public @NonNull ExplorationResult exploreMultiSeed(@NonNull Set<String> seeds, ...)
First line of body: Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, "seeds must not be null");

@NonNull annotation signals to callers and static analysers that null must never be passed; the body then treats that guarantee as insufficient and re-checks it, sending conflicting signals about whether null is a real risk.
detail.suggestion: Remove the Objects.requireNonNull call — the @NonNull annotation is the authoritative contract. If defensive null-guarding is desired at this boundary, replace @NonNull with @Nullable and let the requireNonNull throw be the only enforcement, so annotation and behaviour agree.

..summary: Two public nested records remain in api/model while all sibling entry types are top-level files

evidence: ComparisonResponse.CollocateProfileEntry is declared as a public nested record inside ComparisonResponse.java (line 33), making it navigable only by opening ComparisonResponse.java.
ExamplesResponse.ExampleEntry is declared as a public nested record inside ExamplesResponse.java (line 35) — a trivial 2-field record that mirrors the top-level pattern used by all other entry types.

All other named entry types in api/model are top-level: CollocateEntry, CoreCollocateEntry, DiscoveredNounEntry, EdgeEntry, RelationEntry, RelationListEntry, SeedCollocateEntry each have their own file.

This continues the inconsistency flagged in the previously open issue (now partially resolved — EdgeEntry, CoreCollocateEntry, DiscoveredNounEntry were promoted, but CollocateProfileEntry and ExampleEntry were not).
detail.suggestion: Extract ComparisonResponse.CollocateProfileEntry to CollocateProfileEntry.java and ExamplesResponse.ExampleEntry to ExampleEntry.java (or ExamplesResponse$ExampleEntry.java is also acceptable) in the same api/model package, following the established pattern. Update ComparisonResponse and ExamplesResponse to import those top-level types.

..summary: HttpApiUtils imports ExplorationException from exploration package, violating the stated spi-only boundary for the api layer

evidence: HttpApiUtils.java line 5: import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.exploration.ExplorationException — imports directly from the exploration implementation package
exploration/spi/package-info.java states: 'Service provider interface for semantic-field exploration — the only package the api layer needs to import'
ExplorationException is referenced in ExplorationService Javadoc @throws prose but is not part of any method signature, so it is not visible through the spi contract at the type system level
wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils catches ExplorationException to produce HTTP 503, tying a general-purpose utility to a subsystem-specific exception type
detail.suggestion: Move ExplorationException into the exploration.spi package so it is part of the published contract the api layer is already permitted to import. Alternatively, handle ExplorationException within ExplorationHandlers rather than in the shared HttpApiUtils error wrapper — catch it there and rethrow as an IOException or a boundary-neutral checked exception before wrapWithErrorHandling sees it.

..summary: BlackLab 4.x absent from Maven Central forces a manual pre-build install step and a fragile raw-GitHub-URL fallback

evidence: pom.xml comment (line 113-130): 'BlackLab 4.x is NOT published to Maven Central … REQUIRED FIRST STEP: run ./install-blacklab.sh'
A `blacklab-github-fallback` Maven profile exists that points to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/… with checksumPolicy=warn — no SLA, can break on rate-limit, branch deletion, or repo rename
install-blacklab.sh downloads from GitHub releases and runs `mvn install:install-file` into the local ~/.m2 repo; any CI pipeline without this pre-step fails at dependency resolution
detail.suggestion: Mirror both blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser JARs to a stable private registry (GitHub Packages, Nexus, or Artifactory) and reference that registry in pom.xml. This eliminates the manual script dependency and the unreliable raw-GitHub URL, making standard `mvn clean package` work out of the box for all developers and CI runners.

..summary: jackson-databind carries an explicit version tag that duplicates what jackson-bom already manages

evidence: pom.xml dependencyManagement imports jackson-bom at 2.17.2 (lines 27-32), which already pins jackson-databind to 2.17.2
pom.xml dependencies block also declares jackson-databind with <version>${jackson.version}</version> = 2.17.2 (lines 54-56)
Versions currently agree, so there is no runtime conflict, but the redundant pin means a future BOM upgrade will silently be shadowed by the explicit version unless both are updated in sync
detail.suggestion: Remove the <version> tag from the jackson-databind dependency declaration in pom.xml; let the imported jackson-bom manage the version. This eliminates the dual-maintenance risk and is the standard pattern when a BOM is already in use.

..summary: ExamplesContext.fallbackUsed boolean is encoded as Boolean.TRUE | null in buildExamplesResponse, making false and absent indistinguishable

evidence: ExamplesContext record declares `boolean fallbackUsed` — a plain two-valued type.
buildExamplesResponse passes `ctx.fallbackUsed() ? Boolean.TRUE : null` to ExamplesResponse — the value false is silently mapped to null rather than Boolean.FALSE.

ExamplesResponse.fallback is @Nullable Boolean with @JsonInclude(NON_NULL): when fallbackUsed is false the field is suppressed from the JSON response, making false and 'not set' indistinguishable to clients.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples always passes fallbackUsed=false, so the field is always absent on that path — the asymmetry is not exercised there, but it is exercised in ConcordanceHandlers where a true fallback is possible.
detail.suggestion: Make the encoding honest: either (a) change ExamplesResponse.fallback to a plain boolean and always emit it (false when no fallback was used, true when it was), or (b) keep @Nullable Boolean but remove the boolean→null conversion by passing Boolean.FALSE directly when fallbackUsed is false, relying on @JsonInclude(NON_NULL) only for the genuinely 'not applicable' case (e.g., the exploration path) by omitting the field at call-site rather than through a false→null coercion.

..summary: SketchHandlers returns HTTP 200 for partial sketch failures, requiring callers to inspect response body for errors

evidence: executeRelationQueries catches IOException per-relation, adds to List<String> relationErrors, and returns RelationQueryBatch; callers pass this to buildSketchResponse which sets status='partial' and warnings=[...] but emits HTTP 200.
All exploration handlers (ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore etc.) let IOException propagate to wrapWithErrorHandling which emits HTTP 500/503.

The asymmetry is documented with an inline comment: 'Intentional asymmetry with exploration handlers' but HTTP 200 for partial failure still diverges from conventional REST semantics (HTTP 207 Multi-Status or 500 would be more discoverable).
detail.suggestion: Retain the partial-results design but return HTTP 207 Multi-Status (or HTTP 500 when all relations failed) so callers can detect failure without parsing the body. Alternatively, expose a header (e.g. X-Partial-Response: true) to complement the body-level signal without breaking existing consumers.

..summary: Conditional TODO about migrating HTTP server to Undertow/Jetty has no owner or deadline

evidence: Line 54: '// TODO: migrate to Undertow or Jetty if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed.'
The trigger condition ('if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed') is speculative and may never apply; no owner is named and no deadline exists
detail.suggestion: Either remove the TODO entirely (the current com.sun.net.httpserver choice is well-justified and the comment above already explains it), or convert it to a decision record or issue tracker item with an explicit owner if the scenario is genuinely anticipated. Do not leave it as an ownerless TODO.

..summary: Dead null check in toHitRecord — safeParts never returns null, so the sentinel branch can never execute

evidence: CollocateQueryHelper.toHitRecord line 299: 'String[] parts = BlackLabSnippetParser.safeParts(conc); if (parts == null) { return new HitRecord(…); }'
BlackLabSnippetParser.safeParts always either returns a non-null 3-element array or throws IllegalArgumentException — it has no code path that returns null.

If safeParts throws, the exception propagates uncaught through collectHits and out of executeBcqlQuery's outer try-catch (which only catches InvalidQuery), then surfaces as a 400 Bad Request in wrapWithErrorHandling — incorrect for an infrastructure/data error.
By contrast, executeCqlQuery correctly wraps the same safeParts call in its own try-catch to skip bad concordances, but the collectHits path has no equivalent handler.
detail.suggestion: Remove the dead 'if (parts == null)' branch in toHitRecord. Add a try-catch around the safeParts call in toHitRecord (or inside collectHits) that catches IllegalArgumentException, logs a debug message, and returns a null or skips the record — mirroring the pattern already used in executeCqlQuery.

..summary: ComparisonResponse reuses ExploreResponse.Parameters, inheriting a compare-inapplicable nouns_per field

evidence: ComparisonResponse.parameters is typed as ExploreResponse.Parameters, which includes nouns_per annotated @Nullable Integer.

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildComparisonResponse always passes null for nouns_per: new ExploreResponse.Parameters(relationType, params.topCollocates(), params.minShared(), params.logDiceThreshold(), null).
The compare endpoint never accepts or uses nouns_per, so the field is structurally inapplicable but permanently embedded at this boundary.
detail.suggestion: Extract a shared ParametersBase record (relation, top, min_shared, min_logdice) that both ExploreResponse.Parameters and a new CompareParameters record extend or embed. ComparisonResponse should reference CompareParameters, which omits nouns_per entirely, removing the nullable placeholder from the compare seam.

..summary: ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method carries no @JsonProperty, producing asymmetric serialisation across variants

evidence: The interface declares List<DiscoveredNounEntry> primarySeeds() with no @JsonProperty annotation.
SingleSeed serialises the component accessor as discovered_nouns via @JsonProperty("discovered_nouns") on the record component.

MultiSeed suppresses the interface method with @JsonIgnore and exposes source_seeds via the separate sourceSeeds() accessor — a two-step workaround that relies on a @JsonIgnore to prevent a phantom discovered_nouns key in the multi-seed output.

A consumer iterating polymorphically over ExploreResponse values and invoking Jackson serialisation on the interface type would produce inconsistent or missing JSON keys without reading the per-variant annotations.
detail.suggestion: Remove primarySeeds() from the sealed interface — it exists only to satisfy a shared data-access contract used in tests, but its serialisation semantics are variant-specific. Tests can cast to the concrete type or use the concrete accessors directly. Keeping the interface free of asymmetric serialisation obligations makes the boundary contract honest.

..summary: collocateFrequency in SingleSeedExplorer.identifyCoreCollocates counts noun-sharing, not corpus frequency

evidence: Line 181: Map<String, Integer> collocateFrequency = new LinkedHashMap<>()
Line 185: collocateFrequency.merge(collocate.getKey(), 1, Integer::sum) — increments by 1 per DiscoveredNoun that shares this collocate
This counts how many nouns in the semantic field co-occur with each collocate — not corpus frequency
In contrast, MultiSeedExplorer uses collocateSharedCount for the same concept (in SeedCollocateData), which is precise

In this codebase 'frequency' and 'freq' consistently refer to corpus occurrence counts (wordSketchResult.frequency(), getTotalFrequency, headwordFreq, collocateFreq); this variable breaks that convention
detail.suggestion: Rename collocateFrequency to collocateNounCount or collocateSharedNounCount in identifyCoreCollocates to match the naming used in MultiSeedExplorer and to distinguish it from corpus frequency.

..summary: executeCollocations and executeSurfacePattern use inconsistent naming grammar on QueryExecutor

evidence: executeCollocations: verb + noun-plural — describes the thing returned
executeSurfacePattern: verb + noun-phrase — describes the mechanism, not the thing returned

Both methods do collocate-lookup over an index; their naming should follow the same grammar, e.g. executeCollocations / executeSurfacePatternCollocations, or executeCollocates / executeSurfacePatternCollocates
detail.suggestion: Rename executeSurfacePattern to executeSurfacePatternCollocations (or alternatively rename both to a verb+mechanism grammar: executeFromPattern / executeFromCql). Pick one grammar and apply it to all collocate-lookup methods on the interface.

..summary: GroupStats private record in BlackLabQueryExecutor does not communicate what is grouped or what statistics it holds

evidence: private record GroupStats(Map<String, Long> freqMap, Map<String, String> lemmaPosMap) — name says 'group statistics' but fields reveal it is collocate co-occurrence frequencies plus a lemma-to-POS map
Used in collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups which returns a GroupStats — the method name is more descriptive than the type name
A name like CollocateExtractionResult or CollocateFrequencyData would make the return type self-documenting
detail.suggestion: Rename GroupStats to CollocateExtractionResult or CollocateFrequencyData to reflect what the record holds (co-occurrence frequencies and POS annotations extracted from HitGroups).

..summary: DependencySketchTest uses @Disabled instead of the established guard pattern, leaving executeDependencyPattern untested in CI

evidence: DependencySketchTest annotates the entire class with @Disabled("Requires a live BlackLab index — set CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var and remove @Disabled to run") rather than using Assumptions.assumeTrue() + @Tag("integration") as done in BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest and BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest.

BlackLabQueryExecutorTest adds CI-runnable stub-based guard tests for executeCollocations (null/empty lemma → empty list, null pattern → IAE) but no equivalent tests exist for executeDependencyPattern's guard clauses (empty lemma early-return at line 186, Objects.requireNonNull for deprel).

The headPosConstraint branch in executeDependencyPattern (two distinct BCQL formats with different regex escaping paths) and the full queryAndRankDepCollocates → collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups pipeline have no CI coverage.
detail.suggestion: Remove @Disabled from DependencySketchTest and replace it with Assumptions.assumeTrue(INDEX_PATH != null && new File(INDEX_PATH).exists(), ...) per the pattern in BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest. Additionally, add stub-based guard-clause tests in BlackLabQueryExecutorTest (or a new DependencyPatternGuardTest) that exercise executeDependencyPattern with a null-index executor: null deprel should throw NullPointerException (Objects.requireNonNull), empty lemma should return empty list, and a valid call with a null headPosConstraint vs non-null headPosConstraint should produce distinct BCQL strings (testable by subclassing BlackLabQueryExecutor and capturing the bcql argument).

..summary: @NonNull annotation on exploreMultiSeed seeds parameter contradicted by null-defensive body

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed declares @NonNull Set<String> seeds on line ~195 then immediately calls Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, "seeds must not be null") in the body — if the annotation is honoured by the toolchain, the runtime check is dead code; if the runtime check is needed, the annotation is a false promise
ExplorationService interface declares the same @NonNull seeds parameter without the runtime guard — the two representations carry different implicit trust levels for the same contract
detail.suggestion: Remove Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, ...) and rely solely on the @NonNull annotation, or remove @NonNull and keep the runtime check with a documented reason. Align the interface and implementation on the same trust model. Given the codebase uses JSpecify @NonNull consistently elsewhere without runtime duplicates, removing the redundant requireNonNull is the natural fix.

..summary: WordSketchResult record fields lemma and pos carry no nullability annotations despite a documented UNKNOWN_POS sentinel

evidence: WordSketchResult record: public record WordSketchResult(String lemma, String pos, ...) — both String fields unannotated; UNKNOWN_POS = "unknown" sentinel indicates pos is never null in practice but the record does not express this

CollocateEntry API response record mirrors the same gap: public record CollocateEntry(String lemma, long frequency, double logDice, String pos) — no @NonNull or @Nullable on lemma or pos at the serialization boundary
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates substitutes UNKNOWN_POS when posMap lacks an entry, confirming pos is always non-null at construction time — an invariant the record type could express
detail.suggestion: Annotate WordSketchResult fields with @NonNull String lemma and @NonNull String pos to express the invariant held at construction time, and apply the same to CollocateEntry. This closes the gap between the runtime guarantee (pos is always UNKNOWN_POS or a real tag, never null) and the type-declared contract.

..summary: ExplorationService lives in exploration/spi/ but SketchQueryPort and CollocateQueryPort live flat in query/ alongside the implementation

evidence: exploration/spi/ is a dedicated sub-package containing only ExplorationService.java and package-info.java — the pattern signals that this is the public API boundary the api layer should import.

query/ keeps SketchQueryPort and CollocateQueryPort as top-level files in the same package as BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper, and QueryExecutor — the interface/implementation separation is present in code but not expressed structurally.
The package-info.java for exploration/spi explicitly says 'the only package the api layer needs to import', implying an intentional isolation convention that was not applied to the query subsystem.
detail.suggestion: Either move SketchQueryPort and CollocateQueryPort to a query/spi/ sub-package (mirroring the exploration convention) or move ExplorationService out of exploration/spi/ back into exploration/ (reverting to flat convention). The flat-in-package approach is simpler and already works — moving ExplorationService would reduce asymmetry with less disruption than introducing query/spi/.

..summary: wrapWithErrorHandling's final catch(Exception) silently maps programming errors (NPE, CCE) to HTTP 500, masking bugs

evidence: catch (Exception e) at line 76 logs at ERROR and sends HTTP 500 'Unexpected error: ...' — covers NPE, ClassCastException, AssertionError subclasses, etc.
No rethrow or differentiation between programming-error subtypes and genuinely unexpected runtime conditions.
In local development the error message surfaces in the JSON body, but in production the 500 is indistinguishable from an I/O failure already handled by the IOException clause above it.
detail.suggestion: Re-throw Error subtypes (OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError) uncaught. For RuntimeException, consider catching separately from Exception and logging at a different severity or including a discriminator field in the JSON error body (e.g. 'error_class') so monitoring can distinguish programming bugs from expected I/O failures.

..summary: ExploreResponseAssembler is a pure model-to-DTO translator with only static methods living in api/, which owns HTTP concerns

evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler is declared `final class` with a private constructor and only static methods — it has no HttpExchange references, no HTTP lifecycle state, and imports nothing from com.sun.net.httpserver.
SketchResponseAssembler follows the same pattern: package-private, static-only, no HTTP state.

The api/ package-info explicitly includes 'response assembly' in its scope, and both assemblers are intentionally package-private, meaning the placement is a deliberate design choice, not an accident.
However, a developer looking for where model↔DTO translation lives would need to know to search inside api/ rather than model/ or a dedicated translation layer.
detail.suggestion: If the codebase grows and the number of assemblers increases, consider moving all assemblers into a dedicated api/assembler/ sub-package (parallel to api/model/) to give translation logic a predictable home separate from HTTP-handler code. For now, documenting the co-location rationale in each assembler's Javadoc (e.g. 'Kept package-private in api/ to avoid a single-class sub-package') would remove the ownership ambiguity without requiring a move.

..summary: RELATION_ALIASES static map silently drifts from grammar-config JSON IDs on mismatch

evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES = Map.of('adj_modifier','noun_modifiers', 'adj_predicate','noun_adj_predicates', ...) — values are string literals that must match 'id' fields in the runtime-loaded relations.json

validateAliases(GrammarConfig) logs a warning when a mapped ID is absent from the config but does not throw, so a renamed relation would silently break alias resolution at request time rather than at startup
detail.suggestion: Change validateAliases() to throw IllegalStateException (or at minimum log at ERROR and set a health-check flag) when any alias target is absent from the loaded grammar config, so the mismatch is caught at startup rather than discovered via 404s at request time.

..summary: CROSS_RELATIONAL sentinel string lives in ExplorationHandlers rather than the config/domain layer

evidence: ExplorationHandlers defines private static final String CROSS_RELATIONAL = "cross_relational" and passes it as the relationType argument to buildComparisonResponse.

RelationType and RelationUtils contain no corresponding constant or entry for cross-relational aggregation, so this sentinel is invisible to the domain layer and to callers that follow the config abstraction.
detail.suggestion: Add a CROSS_RELATIONAL constant to RelationType (or RelationUtils) and reference it from ExplorationHandlers. This makes the sentinel part of the shared vocabulary and removes the ad-hoc string from the handler layer.

..summary: model/ root holds only PosGroup (fan-in 4), thinning the package below useful navigability

evidence: model/PosGroup.java is the only non-package-info file in model/; 4 importers span 2 packages (config and exploration)
Fan-in = 4, just below the 5-importer threshold for a shared root type
model/package-info.java documents the sub-package rationale but does not explain why PosGroup stays at the root rather than moving to model/sketch/ or a dedicated model/common/ package
detail.suggestion: Option A (minimal): move PosGroup into model/sketch/ alongside WordSketchResult/CollocateResult, since POS groups are primarily a query/sketch concept, and update 4 import sites. Option B (more explicit): create model/common/ or model/core/ for cross-cutting domain enums and move PosGroup there. Staged plan: (1) git mv model/PosGroup.java model/sketch/PosGroup.java; (2) update package declaration; (3) fix imports in RelationConfig, RelationUtils, CollocateProfileComparator, SemanticFieldExplorer with sed -i 's/word_sketch.model.PosGroup/word_sketch.model.sketch.PosGroup/g' <files>; (4) run mvn test to validate.

..summary: SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest matches URL path segment "dep" with a raw string literal instead of RelationType.DEP.label()

evidence: SketchHandlers.java:59: if (parts.length > 1 && "dep".equals(parts[1])) — the string "dep" is the exact value of RelationType.DEP.label() but is not referenced through it
RelationType.DEP.label() is already used in the same file for other purposes (e.g. buildDepRelationEntry), making the inconsistency visible on inspection
If RelationType.DEP.label() were changed (e.g. to "dependency"), the URL routing branch would silently stop matching and all /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep requests would fall through to the wrong handler
detail.suggestion: Replace "dep".equals(parts[1]) with RelationType.DEP.label().equals(parts[1]) to couple the routing to the canonical enum label and make future label changes safe.

..summary: viz/ is a single-file package whose sole production consumer is api/VisualizationHandlers

evidence: viz/ contains only RadialPlot.java (247 LOC) plus package-info.java
Fan-in = 1 production importer: api/VisualizationHandlers.java; no other package references it
RadialPlot generates SVG geometry; VisualizationHandlers wraps it in HTTP responses — distinct concerns, but with no other consumer the current packaging overhead exceeds the payoff
detail.suggestion: If RadialPlot is not expected to grow into a larger visualization subsystem, collapse it into api/ as a package-private rendering helper, or move it under api/viz/ to keep the SVG-generation boundary visible without a top-level package. Staged plan: (1) git mv viz/RadialPlot.java api/RadialPlot.java; (2) update package declaration to pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api; (3) update VisualizationHandlers import; (4) run mvn test. If future growth of a visualization library is planned, leave as-is and document intent in viz/package-info.java.

risk_flags.message: Strict/lenient gap is 1.7 pts with 297 wontfix issues
